key_insights.insight: Growing up without representation and being constantly told to assimilate can create a false sense of self, contributing to identity crises later in life.
key_insights.quote: I grew up at a time when there weren't many black people on television, when there weren't many black images on television or anywhere. And I think that seriously, I don't want to say put me to disadvantage, but I grew up with a false sense of myself.

key_insights.insight: Repressing racist experiences can lead to a build-up of resentment and anger, which can manifest in destructive ways.
key_insights.quote: And I think that was just a buildup of resentment. Of having to live in this place where, yeah, everyone's calling me this name. Everyone thinks it's funny. And I'm laughing, but there's a build-up of resentment that he then takes out on his family.

key_insights.insight: Psychosis is not always a purely biological phenomenon but can also be influenced by environmental factors and cultural experiences, particularly for marginalized groups in Western societies.
key_insights.quote: What I realized is that black people, there's way less mental health in Africa amongst black community...But there's more when they are transmitted to a Western culture. So there's more mental health, episodes of mental health in England amongst the black community and in America amongst the black community.

key_insights.insight: The rejection and 'othering' that marginalized groups experience can have profound psychological effects, leading to a destabilized sense of self.
key_insights.quote: It's constantly been sort of, it's growing up in a period where you're othered, where you're in fear and not understanding who you are was destabilizing, I think.

key_insights.insight: Acknowledging and addressing past trauma, even years later, is essential for rebuilding a strong sense of self and identity.
key_insights.quote: Even though it happened 30 years ago, I'm only now just dealing with it because I only found the records... I just, I've spent the last 30 years in this sort of cocoon, not really dealing with a lot of this stuff.

key_insights.insight: It's important to actively work to control what you can in your life, and accept what you can't, to prevent resentment from building.
key_insights.quote: Control what I can control. And don't...If I don't get a job, I don't get a job. I can't... There's nothing I can do about that. I can control how I feel about it. And just think it wasn't for me.

key_insights.insight: There is shame attached to mental health and psychosis, and particularly to being sectioned, because society doesn't discuss this topic often.
key_insights.quote: There's a shame attached to particularly psychosis and particularly to being taken away. There's a shame attached to it.

actionable_advice.advice: Seek therapy, especially with a therapist who shares your cultural background, to address identity-related insecurities and fears.
actionable_advice.how_to: Find a therapist who specializes in cultural identity and race-related issues and begin regular sessions to explore these topics.

actionable_advice.advice: Practice self-care by controlling what you can control in your life and accepting what you can't.
actionable_advice.how_to: Identify areas in your life where you have agency and focus on making positive changes in those areas. Practice acceptance for things outside of your control.

actionable_advice.advice: For those around you, if you sense something is wrong, offer support and open communication to prevent deterioration of their mental state.
actionable_advice.how_to: Notice changes in behavior, listen empathetically, and encourage professional help if someone is behaving out of character or fixated on something.

memorable_stories: Story of a brick being thrown through the window of his childhood home.
An experience of walking into the Leeds United football end and being subjected to racist abuse.
The account of his psychotic episode, including hearing the voice of Martin Luther King and being sectioned.
The account of reading his medical records for the first time.
contrarian_claims: That sometimes trauma has a purpose.
It's okay to be vulnerable, and not to adhere to the image of the strong black man.
episode_title: The Diary Of A CEO with Steven Bartlett - David Harewood: The Chilling Story Of How A Hollywood Star Lost His Mind
